California
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
Septenber 4, 1990
Bob l,lulhol}and, Campaign Manager
Yes on 128 - Big Green
926 J Street, #L4OO
Sacramento, CA 958L4
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-90-552
Dear ltr. Mulholland:
You are seeking advice as campaign manager on behalf of the
rrYes on 128 - Big creen campaigntt ('tBig creen campaigntt) regarding
its duties and responsibi l ities under the dj-sclosure of najor
funding sources provisi,ons of the PoLitical Reform Act (the
trlggtt; . l'
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
letter of August 22 t L99O, and in a telephone conversation on Au-
gust 28, 1990. The advice is prospective in its application; the
commissj.on does not provide advice concerning past conduct.
(Regulation L8329 , copy enclosed.)
OUESTION
Big creen Carnpaign has established a fundraising cornmittee
and an idvertisenent committee. Funds received by the fundraising
committee are provided to the advertisement cornmittee.
(1) Are contributj,ons to the fundraising committee
considered contributions for purposes of disclosure under sections
84501-84508 ('rProposition 105rr ) ?
(2) Are the funds given by the fundraising committee to the
advertisement cornmittee ionsidered to be rrfromrr California for
purposes of Proposition 105 when a high percentage of the funds
ieceivea by the fundraising committee come from out of state
sources bul are deposited into the fundraising committee's cali-
fornia bank account?
(3) Does the structuring of the flow of funds in this manner
avoid'any disclosure obligations under Proposition 105 altogether?
1 coararrr."nt code Sections 810oo-9L015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. Comnission
iegulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section
18600, et seq.- A11 references to regutations are to Title 2, Di-
vision o ot €tre California code of Regulations'
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 807 . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322'5660


FiIe No. A-90-552
Paqe 2
CONCLUSION
(1) and (3) The source of funds received by tlre fundraj.sing
committee is to be considered in determining what disclosure obli-
gations, if any, under Proposition 105 apply to the Biq Green cam-
paign's advertising.
(2) Because the source of funds received by tfte fundraising
conmittee is to be considered in deterrnining what disclosure obli-
gations under Proposition 105 appty, the fact that the fundraising
6onrnittee deposits such funds into its California bank account is
irrelevant for purposes of determininq whether or not the funds
are received trfromrr California sources.
rACTS
The Big Green Campaign has established two committees - one
that buys advertising and one that acts as an |tintermediaryrr for
fundraising purposes. The two committees share the same sPonsors
and campailn- address, are controlled by the same carnpaign firm,
and act in concert. The fundraising cornmittee is responsible for
raising money for the canpaign, and the advertisement committee
;r1I rnike the actual advertisement purchases. rt is understood
that the funds received by the fundraising cornmittee are provided
to the advertisement connittee and then used for the purchase of
broadcast and print media advertising.
ANALYSIS
Sections 84501 through 84514 were added to the Act as a re-
sult of the enactment by ahe voters of Proposition 105 in the No-
vember 19g8 election. 
ihese provisions, effective January 1, 1990
impose specific disclosure requirenents on committees which pay
foi or ai.rthorize an advertisenent in support of or opposition to
an initiative. 
The disctosure requirement is applicable only to
an advertisement the content of which is more than 50 Percent de-
"ot"a t" one initiatlrrq2 lSection 84512), paid for and authorized
uv anv comnittee thich has rnade expenditures of at least $50'000.
liection 84502.) when required, the disclosure consists of an
icknowledgrment, contained-in the advertisement, of the major fund-
ing sourc6s of the organization which authorized and paid for the
advertisernent .
Because Proposition 128 on the November, 1990 baI1ot is a
"gualified state initiative,tr 
the -provis.ions of Proposition 1o5
aie applicable to the requisite advertisements in support of or in
opposition to the ProPosition.
Inthefactsyouhaveprovided,theadvertisernentcommittee
[authorizes" the advertisen-ent on behalf of Proposition 128 and is
2  An initiative is defined as ila
measure or a qua■ified statement
8450■)。
qualif ied statewide initiative
referendum neasurer' ( Sect ion


File No. A-90-552
Page 3
therefore subject to the discLosure reguirements-concerning its
major funding sources pursuant to Section 84507.3 Because the
fundraislng cornmittee provides all of the funds to the advertise-
ment committee to enable it to purchase advertisenents, the fund-
raisingr committee is clearly a rrrnajor funding sourcerr of the ad-
vertisement committee under Section 84507.
The basis of your questions is in what nanner, if any, the
fundraising committee is otherwise included in the Proposition 105
disclosure 
- requirements . It is our conclusion that the deternina-
tion of the appropriate disclosure categories and obligations for
the advertisenents requires an exarnination of the fundinq sources
of the fundraising committee, for the following two reasons.
First, both the advertisement and fundraising committees have
as their sole purpose the Big Green Initiative. 
They are control-
Ied and managed by the same people, share the same sponsors, have
the same canpaign address, and \.rork in concert- They are, in
fact, one and the same for all purposes except for the specific
activity in which they engage.
we have previously advised that Proposition 1o5's disclosure
requirernent does not call for disclosure of the major donors of a
major funding source required to be disclosed under section 84507.
The Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. A-90-336 (copy enclosed) , con-
cLuded that a comrnittee could constitute a rnajor funding source
for another committee which has authorized an advertisement. This
3 this section reads as foll,ows:
5 8{507. Disclosure of uajor trundiDg sources.
Any advertisement authorized by a conmittee shaLl include a
statement that each of the following, where applicable, is a major
funding source:
(a) Any industry which is both the largest industry con-
tributioi to Lhe commiltee and whose cornbined contributions to the
committee are five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) or more, or
are fifty 
thousand dollars ($5o,oo0) or nore and constitute 25
percent or rnore of all contributions.
(b) A person whose contributions to the cornmittee are one
hundred thousand doll.ars ($loo,o0o) or nore and who is the larqest
contributor.
(c) Corporations as a group when their conbined contribu-
tions to the conmittee are one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000)
or Dore and constitute 50 percent or more of all contributions,
and unions as a group when their conbined contributions to the
conmittee are one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) or more, and
constitute 50 percent or nore of all contributions.
(d) out-of-state contributors as a group, when
bined contributions to the committee are one hundred
lars (91oo,ooo) or nore, and constitute 508 or more
butions .
their con-
thousand doL-
of all contri-


File No. A-90-552
Page 4
advice lras premised on the understanding that the tvro committees
were separale and distinct entities. I
Here, hob/ever, the two committees are seParate and distinct
entities in naure only, established for the same general policy
purpose and rrhose activities are managed and conducted by the same
individuals. 
The situation, in reverse, is analogous to that in
which the Act requires that contributions made by more than one
person be cumulated and the persons be treated as a single con-
tributor. 
(see Requlation 18531.55 and olson Advice Letter, No'
A-90-302, copies enclosed.) Contributions to the fundraising com-
rnittee are in effect contributions to the advertisement committee,
and vice versa.
To conclude othen ise and perrnit the advertisement committee
to list only the fundraising committee as its najor funding source
would compromise the Act's purpose of achieving fu1I and accurate
disclosure. 
(Section 81002(a).) The cotnmission is under an obli-
gation to implement the intent, and not simply the literal 
lan-
guage, of the Act. 
(See Diane Watson. et aI. v. FPPC (a99O) 21-7
Caf.app.3d 1059.) As such, because the trro committees are virtu-
atty inaistinguishable for alL practical purposes, it is.our con-
clusion that ttre disclosure required by Proposition 105 is to be
determined by an evaluation of the contributions received by both
the advertisernent committee and the fundraising committee.
Second, you have indicated that the fundraising committee
acts as an itinternediary" for funds for the Big Green campaign,
and that all contributions are given for the support of the ini-
tiative. 
A committee nay act as an internediary for the receipt
of contributions for another committee. (Robeck Advice Letter,
No. I-90-448, copy enclosed.) However, an intermediary is re-
quired to disclose the true source of any contributions, and the
iecipient is required to disclose both the interrnediary and the
true contributor. 
(Sections 84302t 84302.5; Regulation 18432.5,
copy enclosed. )
If, therefore, the fundraising committee acts as an j,nterme-
diary for purposes of raising funds for the advertisement commit-
tee, the fundraising comrnittee is required to disclose the names
of the true contributors, as well as itseJ.f , to the advertisement
cornnrittee. with this infornation, the advertisement cornmittee
would then be in the position to determine its obJ'igations, if
any, with respect to the Proposition 105 disclosure requirements-
4 rh" factual reference for this concl,usion is discussed in the
Hiltachk Advice Letter, EIlpEB r concerning the Pete Wilson for
Governor - 1990 cornrnittee and the crine victims for Justice, Yes
on 115 committee.
- This regulation is currently in the rulenaking process, and
although not presently effective, it does express the Cornmission's
policy concerning curnulation of contributions.


File No. A-90-552
Page 5
In either instance, the fact the contributions to the fund-
raisinq conmittee cone iron outside California and are then depos-
ii"a-iit" 
the conmitteers California bank account is j,rrelevant,
1""."." ihe contributors and the amounts they contribute must be
"*i.i""a 
to EEtErrninE wfrether, and which, Proposition 1o5 disclo-
=o."-"lii.g"tions are applicabie. It is- from l'here the contribu-
ti""= ""16, and not to- ihere the contributions are deposited, that
deterrnines disclosure under section 845o7'
To recapitulate, the funding sources of the fundraising con-
nittee-rnust Le exaroinea to deternine if any Proposition 1o5 dis-
"i"="." is requj'red in Big Green campaign. advertising' 
This is
.r""".=.ry beciuse (1) the absence of distinctions betlreen the two
conunitte6's prrapo="t, control, sponsors, addresses, and coordina-
tion requirei tirat the two committees be treated as one, and (2)
ifr" t,r"d."ising cornmittee, s status as an intermediary requires
that its sourc6s of contributions be disclosed to the advertise-
nent committee.
we are continuing to refine our advice concerning Proposition
ros ana are in the pi5cess of drafting further regulations to im-
;i;r;;a il= pi""iti^"".. rhe advice you have been provided in this
i"ii"i -r"V 
"ir..,g" if the Commission adopts a regulation or regula-
iion=, or if suisequent advj-ce is provided, which reaches a dif-
tErence concfusion-from that suggested in this letter'
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
."q..,"-t"d. 
If you have any firrther questions regarding this nat-
tef, please contact ne at (916) 322-590L'
sincerely,
scott HaIlabrin
Acting
l
th
Counsel,, LegaI
